speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, Jerry Shane. You were saying that your mother and your brother disappeared from the ghetto. Can you tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back to the ghettos-- I usually would return, come and go-- I found no one of my family, except the frame houses were flat-- no frame houses. And I seen Polish men, women, and children with sacks and looking amongst the debris. They were there looking for Jews or valuables. And Polish, Jewish, and German police all doing the same-- looking for Jews hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: I approached one of the Jewish policeman named Lolek, who's father had a tavern in town. He was with another Jewish policeman unknown to me. And I ask him where the whereabouts of the Jewish people in the ghetto? He grabbed me by my wrist and tried to turn me in [INAUDIBLE]. I knew the consequences. I bit him under his wrist, and I took a run for it. His only weapon would have been a whistle, nightstick, and an armband indicating he is a Jewish policeman, a kap.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took chase after me. His blowing of his whistle was to alert all the other people there's a Jew in vicinity. I ran-- I ran away from him. He couldn't catch up with me. After the war, I met him in an orphanage. When I was in an orphanage in my hometown, he came dressed as a Polish officer with his brother and sister. And there were many Jewish former soldiers in the hospitals nearby. He came to visit us children from the-- who survived the Holocaust in that orphanage [INAUDIBLE]. And we put on a show for the soldiers. And I turned him in to the authorities with this story that happened. His sister and his brothers-- he was being taken away by the Jewish soldiers, who were only in the pajamas and their house coats. And they-- they're pleading to release their brother because he had to do what he had to do to survive. And how I came back into the ghetto was I was tending cow-- a cow for one of the peasants. And I overheard-- a Jewish lady from a nearby shtetl, a little town, came to do business with this lady who I worked for. And I overheard her from behind the door that she was telling this peasant lady who I was working for. So what happened [INAUDIBLE] that all the Jews were taken away, because before the night before, the same night-- where this following morning the lady showed up, the Jewish lady, told what happened to my hometown ghetto-- said, I had a dream. In the dream, was my mother riding on a horse-drawn wagon loaded full of bundles. Bundles would be like made of a scarf or something making four corners tied together. And on the wagon was my mother and two German soldiers on a horse-drawn wagon. I tried to board the horse-drawn wagon. But my mother chased me off the wagon saying, I don't belong to-- on that wagon. I was very angry at her. I never dreamt about my mother ever again in my life. And I came back to the ghetto as a result. As I've told you before, I found what happening. And I was hiding behind-- as the policeman was chasing me, I hid behind a-- a gate on the outside of the ghetto near-- close to the railroad station. Two Polish boys-- young teenagers about 16,17-- came to urinate behind the gate. They found me there hiding, accused me of being a Jew. I chased them off with a little bayonet I had because I was playing in the field tending cows. And I scared them off like. And I went to the railroad station in Otwock, going to be taking the train going back to the peasant who I was staying with. And I fell asleep on a bench to be awakened at 12 o'clock. Train just arrived from the nearby villages in the direction where I was tending the cow. And there were many people sitting at the [INAUDIBLE]-- black marketeers, Gentile black marketeers. heading to the direction for Warsaw. Later to have discovered-- so they used to disembark on the train in Otwock. So this is where they'd go with the train directly to Warsaw with the goodies they did business with. The gendarmes would liquidate all the stuff they had. 4 o'clock train, electric train, would come. And there was [INAUDIBLE]-- it used to stop at every station before Warsaw. So to beat out the Germans from taking away their goods, they used to sleep it from 12 o'clock to 4 o'clock. I discovered a way to do so. There was a bookcase. In the daytime, it was a newspaper stand. And behind the bookcase was, like a-- was like a counter, also in front of the bookcase. And behind the bookcase was a radiator on tall legs. When a bookcase used to be closed-- this newspaper stand used to be closed-- I crawled behind the bookcase and underneath the radiator on a marble floor, I used to sleep there. I improvised a way of coming and going every night time to come. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still coming back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was no longer-- no ghetto, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we move on to your experiences on the train, is there anything else that you remember about the time that you spent in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. As I mentioned before, children would go around begging. [SPEAKING POLISH] As I said, my little brother was showing off with me how we accumulated a little piece of bread without eating and brought it home. Children lying dead on the street. Grown men-- at least my brother and my father died with dignity, sleeping in their bed. And I remember my mother. The black marketeers used to pile loaves of bread on a stand on the street. People who had valuables, in exchange for a loaf of bread, would exchange for the loaf of bread to these Jewish black marketeers. Although they risked their life to go on bringing-- [INAUDIBLE]. One man I remember grabbed the loaf of bread. He's shoving the loaf of bread down his mouth, and the men, the businessmen, tried to retrieve his loaf of bread. The man didn't give a damn how he was being beaten up to retrieve the bread. He just kept shoving it down his mouth. My mother took me in one of those bakeries. She took me in. And she shoved a challah underneath my coat, which left a lasting memento. But I realize now, when others are dying, you could steal or take it. And it effected me very much, my mother stealing a loaf of bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the ghetto, my mother decided to send me to go to her home village on train. I had no money. I had no identification papers to prove that I'm not Jewish. I spoke with a Yiddish accent. And she gave me direction with huge pronunciation of the cities. I went to board the train. Snuck onto the train, passenger train. I headed to Warsaw. Warsaw-- I changed trains on the advice of my mother to go to Siedlce, the city where I was born. And I wound up in Siedlce. And I was supposed to proceed to Sokolov and then go to a nearby village and look up my-- my family who had a farm. But there were no communication between nobody, because we didn't know-- no telephones, no anyway way to get in touch with nobody. We didn't know their fate. We just remembered what happened before, where they had food on their farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I managed to get to Siedlce. And as I got to Siedlce, when I look up my roots, I don't even remember how I had lived there before. I was born in that city. And I start walking the streets and looking around all over the place. I see two gendarmes stopping people and asking for identification papers. I got scared, and I headed back to the railroad station platform.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Pole of German descent, known as Volksdeutsche, who was the station master, he asked me for my ticket. And I start telling him my mother is on this waiting train now. And [INAUDIBLE] I'm going to miss my mother. I have no ticket. She has my ticket. He recognized my Jewish accents, Yiddish accent. He promptly grabbed me by my wrist and tried to arrest me and pulling, with one hand holding on to me the other hand pulling for his pistol. And I knew the consequences. There's an [INAUDIBLE] sentenced to death for Jews, regardless how old I was then-- 8, 9, 10-- whatever age I was. And I start screaming and crying, my mother's train is leaving! My mother's train is leaving! The train left, of course. It was an imaginary story. I didn't-- there was no mother over there. And the people congregated around me, Gentile people. And two ladies believed my story. Because I was crying they couldn't detect my accent. And they-- he arrested those two ladies. Hauled them off to the jail nearby-- a little prefabricated little cell. And as he hauling them off, the other people took charge of me, put me on the-- on a train heading back to Warsaw. Told the conductor in charge of the train my dilemma. And the conductor felt sorry for me. He told the black marketeers on that train my dilemma. And everybody was giving me all kind of goodies. And I kept-- the more goodies I was getting, the more I was screaming, and hollering, and crying. He transferred me to another conductor heading toward-- to my hometown. I continued the same act. I came home. I told my mother what I did. And she send me back, and I did the similar. She said go back and do the same thing again. I lived on trains like this maybe five, four, five, or six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is what you began doing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Riding the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after you were orphaned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still kept coming into the ghetto from the trains, too. But this was my first introduction to trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is what you continued after you were left alone and the ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liquidated, that's right. I was continued doing this the same thing and various things. First, I used to see a village to my liking. I used to slow the train. First, I actually stopped the train. I didn't know how to pull the brakes properly. So when I pulled the breaks in between the cars, all the people used to fall on top of each other, because the trains are only standing room. And they used to chase them, because I'd jump off the train, and I'd start running towards the nearest village just for handouts. And people would chase after me from the passengers, the black marketeers, because they used to falling, almost get killed. And then I learned how to slow the train down by pulling between the two cars that little handle-- the red handle-- and the steam comes out. So instead of all the way, I pull it part of the way. And as a recreation for myself, I used to sit right on the roof of the train. The train was going, and I was bored sitting just on the roof. So I used to jump from car to car. But I learned that the best way of jumping from car to car, you don't jump forward towards the locomotive, because the winds will blow you between the two cars. You backwards-- the wind blows you backwards. And I used to jump from car to car. And I used to enjoy myself [INAUDIBLE]. And this was part of my enjoyment. And I used to see a village to my liking. I used to go on begging. I used to go on begging. I landed many get jobs of-- from peasants, especially in the springtime. Say, a little boy like you shouldn't go around begging. You should get yourself a job. And they gave me a job tending one cow. I did a very good job. They sent me home for a birth certificate. I couldn't produce it. I had no home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, I used to see many a Polish children run away from homes-- boys. And I used to try to steal from them a birth certificate, hoping they would have a birth certificate from-- with them, since they  had away from home. I used to say to them, you run away from home? They say, oh sure. And they had the best clothes. The only suit probably they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made a deal with them to go with me in the washroom. We'd trade clothes. My clothes were infested full of lice. And I try on the clothes. And I didn't care. I felt guilty at times. But I felt they'll go home to their mother, and then she going to wash it for them-- the clothes. I never got nowhere to go home. And of course I also look for birth certificates. And this was my way to show them-- as a reward to me to show them how to live on the trains. I didn't stick around with them too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I met one boy, and he was much bigger than me and much older than me. And it was almost in the wintertime. And I went with him to the wash room. I told him, he was looking for a job? He said, yeah. So I took him to the nearest village. On the way to the village on a train, we went to the wash room. And I wanted to make sure that he's not Jewish, because if he would be Jewish, why take advantage of him? So I said, let's see who has a bigger penis. Let's measure it. So he pulled out his penis. And by gosh, he's not Jewish at all. So he said, let me see yours. I said, we better get out of here before the conductor catches us here. And I ask him if he has a birth certificate. Sure. So let me see it. He shows me his birth certificate. I make sure I know where he put it. We finally arrived. I took him through. I decided which village shall I take him to and to lose him and steal his birth certificate from his pocket. So I took him through fields, through forests. So he won't find his way back to the train. Lose him completely. And we did. I did so. When I arrived at a peasant's house, and the peasant bawled me out. Where the hell where you the whole summer? I needed you, and you didn't come back. I send you off to get a birth certificate, and you never come back. I said, this is cousin. He has a birth certificate. He said, I don't need you both, neither one of you now. So he let us sleepover on the floor, on the cement floor. And at night when the boy was sleeping, he took this clothes off. And I took not his money. I just took his birth certificate and stuck it in my shoe. And on the way back to the train, I've conveniently lost him on the-- in the village. It was a big, funny story how I lost him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to the nearest village. I used that birth certificate to a man who used to be a bootlegger. And he had lots of food. And this peasant man who was a bootlegger used to go hunting for rabbits. And there was a lot of food in his house. One day, the German surrounded the village and the nearby forest looking for a family named Meyers. The man was a Jewish man who was married to a Gentile family. There were, like, three or four sons, the man they had. And the Germans were looking for them. Chasing them. The shooting was going on. And in the same morning, my-- my boss said to me, I know. He knows I'm Jewish. And he's going to turn me into the Germans. And I said to him, if you turn me over to the Germans-- I said, while the Germans are still looking for Jews, for those Meyer brothers, I said, my family is in the forest over here. And they know where I'm at. And if they come back, and they're going to find me not here, they're going to kill you, and your wife, and your child. He has one little daughter. The wife started crying on this. He didn't believe my story. So I said, give me my birth certificate and I'll go. That's all I want from you. He gave me my birth certificate, and I continued. My name was then, [? Joseph ?] [? Wagemius ?] [? Matushak. ?] [INAUDIBLE] like Jerry Walton [? Matushak. ?] I didn't use it too often because I was afraid. He was born like 1923, 1922. So he was much older than me-- much older. He was a big guy, not even a kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know when you were riding the trains, didn't you need a ticket to board the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to sneak in on the trains. Today when I go on with the airplanes, I look at myself. I say, my gosh, would I have had fun over here sneaking onto the plane with no reserved seatings. I get my reserved seating. And I wouldn't ride on the roof-- of course not. But I would manage very well to get on to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get caught sneaking onto the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. They just kick me off the train. That's all. Take me off one way. I come back the other way. And I-- I-- I did it for five, six years. I lived like this, selling candies on the train. And I used to go around begging. There were many professional entertainers on the train, from opera singers and musicians entertain the black marketeers. And as I was going around begging-- as I go around begging, they only gave me two potatoes-- the farmer, the peasant-- or a piece of bread. And a man can't live by bread alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would go into business, because there were many boys and men selling candy, charms, cigarettes, in the beginning. Several years later, it wouldn't have been allowed to be sold on trains. So I wanted to go into business myself selling candy charms. So I needed money. I had no money. And so I composed my song for myself, which was song composed was that the black marketeers are coming to the peasants and giving all their valuables to the peasant, while the peasant is stuffing his mattress full of money instead of with straw and wearing galoshes with white gloves, throwing out manure with the white gloves. And the gendarmes are liquidating the stuff from the-- from the black marketeers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I sang the songs on the train-- with somebody else's melody, of course, and I passed my hat, and I collected enough money, it took me about a week not to eat to save enough money, collect enough. And then I followed some boys to the candy factory. And many times, I followed them straight home. I didn't know where they were going after the day is over. And I finally found a candy factory in Warsaw. And then I found another candy factory outside of Warsaw, candy which in skinnier. And I started selling candy charms on the train. And I became so prosperous on the train selling candy charms, because I used to sell my candy charms at night when the other boys were sleeping. And that's when the time-- the activity for the black marketeers was at night, naturally. So I didn't have no competition.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day I became so greedy, I invaded some other boy's territory on another train. Going in a different direction. I had never been there before in that direction. And the boys told me, that's-- that's our railroad. That's our train, to get off, not to [INAUDIBLE] selling candy charms.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't listen to them. They bank robbed me, took everything away from me, and beat me up. So I had to go again, singing songs and begging. I went back in business little by little.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spent your days or sometimes your nights on the train. When did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept wherever I could and whenever I could. I used to ride the train, let's say. And the train was full of people at night. And the people standing next to each other, body to body. Can you imagine me standing next to between those people, and lice infested? And I used to sleep by standing up next to them. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I slept also from 12 o'clock train until 4 o'clock in the morning behind that bookcase underneath the radiator there. Almost every night. I improvised a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Otwock in the railroad station-- Otwock. Christmas time is a very, very-- every time Christmas comes around, it's very emotional to me. Christmas day, the peasants are baking goodies. Erev yontif they are preparing. They're very generous and very charitable. Catholic religion is very similar, as far as charity is concerned, as the Jewish religion-- tzedakah. And they are very generous, and they bake. As they bake, they gave me all kind of cakes, all kind of goodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this time, I collected lots of goodies. And I figured, I didn't eat them. How much can a man eat? So I went back to an earlier train I took. And I sat myself down on a bench near my bookcase, facing my bookcase. And I'd fall asleep, like on the bench. And then a Pole of German descent, a Volksdeutsche, named Schlicht, Herr Schlicht, walked in wearing his SS uniform. He had different uniforms. He was the man-- he was the man liquidating Otwock's ghetto. He was a man of small stature, size. He had a German shepherd dog with him. And he passed by my bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was customary for a Jew, pointing with his little finger, come, come, Juden. Come, come, Jew. And I responded, I'm not Jew, in Polish, because I was pretending that I don't speak German. I don't understand German. Yiddish and German is very close. And as he went back and forth like this, and started coaching me to come to him, I took a run for it. The German shepherd dog knocked me to the ground, tore my-- off my shoulders some of my schmatta I was liquidating, people were stealing from people's houses. And he had me arrested. Two Polish policemen came after midnight and they took me to the Polish, to the station-- to the police station. And usually, customary they do not take you to no police station. They execute you right on the spot. This time he couldn't believe himself. In the middle of winter, Christmas time, that a Jewish child would be still surviving by himself. It was the middle of the war. There was no ghettos no more. They were nonexistent-- those Jewish people. They are still looking for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took me to police station to interview me. The two policemen take me to the police station. I told them the same story that I told Mr. Schlict, Herr Schlict and the chief of the Polish police.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, my mother is a-- I have a stepmother. She went to my home, to the village where I worked to collect a sack of potatoes for my pay for the week-- for the summer. And I'm waiting for her to come back. My father is a prisoner of war in Germany. We have about a dozen children we have at home. And said, when she doesn't find me there waiting for her, she'll kill me. And I told this to the Chief of Police, and he told this to Herr Schlict. They looked at each other, and they asked me where I lived. So I told them [POLISH] 8, which was my real address from before the war. He said, you must be a bandit. You must understand, a bandit-- they didn't kill bandits. What a sigh of relief, calling me a bandit.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he asked me my last and first name, I gave him [POLISH]. [POLISH] was happened to be the station master of that-- in Otwock. He said, are you related to [POLISH] the rail master? I say, yeah, sure. He's my uncle. I-- I wanted to be related to somebody who's not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took my shoelaces away. Throw everything I had into my cap, and they put me in a little cell. The cell maybe 10 by 10. It had, like, a little platform and a bunch of Polish men, a gypsy in the station there, in the little cell. And one of the men happened to be a man who spoke Yiddish fluently, because here was my hometown, [INAUDIBLE] the Jewish people grew up and accusing me that I'm a Jew. He knows me, he told everybody. And then I told everybody my story. The gypsy believed my story and kept cursing my stepfather to coming to bail me out. My gypsy [INAUDIBLE] thought better. He tells fortunes. And he was the only one who sympathized with me. And I kept crying, screaming, going on there. They gave us a little cabbage soup through a little window in the door. I threw the cabbage soup back. I didn't want to die. I want to live. I was scared to die. Sure enough, they brought in about five or six Jews-- butchers, big guys, men handcuffed each other. Except one was not handcuffed. He was by himself. He was the older one. And they were hiding in a station master's nearby village or station in his house. They arrested him, too. They had him over there, too, the old man. And the daughter also was arrested. She was not with us in the cell. And then when they called, all Jews be ready to come out. So I went in the washroom. I could see [INAUDIBLE]. You figure, you'll have exclusive on everything. It's not exclusive. Somebody does the same thing. I've been telling that story before [INAUDIBLE] became the movie. Where I pull in the wash room to make sure they don't check me if I'm circumcised, I pull a string on and tied my forehead skin, and I urinated on it. It swell up. But I didn't have no pain because I kept screaming. And all the Jews, they called them out. And they went out, and I stayed behind. Because I'm not Jewish. I pretended to be. The Polish man who spoke Yiddish pushed me toward the door. And the warden, the policeman, grabbed me and dragged me to the office again. I faced again Herr Schlict and the Chief of Police, the Polish Chief of Police. As they did before, promised me everything they're going to do for my family. Why are you pretending to be-- we know you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they summoned to one of the Jewish men who was not handcuffed to talk to me in Yiddish. I did the proper things I should be doing as a Gentile. I hit the man as hard as I could reach up to him. He was taller than me. And start kicking him and hitting him. You dirty Jew. Why are you excusing me? I'm not a Jew. You dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Herr Schlict looked at his Chief of Police, and they told me to go. He gave me all my things back. And I-- as I'm walking out, the policeman who walked me to the door kicked me in my rear end. Saying, I know you are Jewish. You were lying along.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I'm walking alongside the railroad tracks leading back to the railroad station, I seen a man following me amongst many people walking on the side, but he looked differently. He was not a Pole, obviously. Because I became so observant, I notice anybody who looks-- like, even today, I notice people. What they look like, how they dress. I'm very observant. Do they look Jewish? Don't they look Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started running. He started running. Lucky for me, a freight train was coming. I hopped over the barbed wire-- it still now is there. It was there a few years back. And I jumped the barbed wire over, and I hopped the train. It was moving not as fast as a regular train would. It was a freight train. And I stopped-- slowed down the train between stations. And I went off for [INAUDIBLE]. Even I was a small child, I knew they're going to put-- call the next station. They're going to be waiting for me. So that was my next adventure I had on the trains. Many other adventures I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that every day you were living in fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid. I knew, why me? Why me? I know I'm going to eventually-- I didn't care of eventually, because I didn't think of eventually. I just lived from second to second. It was almost like an adventure for me, because the mischievous things I used to do. For example, I-- going back in the ghetto now-- I want to rebel against my mother because everybody died already. So when I had-- I rolled up a newspaper. And I'd light up the newspaper, puffing on it. Flames would shoot up and thinking my mother should beat me up. She didn't touch me, wouldn't beat me up because I was the man of the house. I told the same story to my two children and my late wife, Helen, while riding in the car. We had a German Shepherd in the back. The children sitting in the back. As we're going to a field-- we are near Chicago. We used to go joy riding all four-- five of us, including the dog. And so I seen-- over a little bridge we're coming over there was a boy-- young boy and girl naked, having sex, evidently. So my German shepherd dog looked down at them. And I said-- and my children are sitting in the car. I don't know if they noticed or not. So I proceeded telling to my late wife, Helen, children who have no supervision get themselves in trouble. They get themselves pregnant. And then I proceeded to telling her about the newspaper adventure. And I said, that's what I used to do with my mother-- rebel because there was no father at home. You see, there's no father here. She will come home pregnant. As I got through saying this, my older daughter Marcia says to me, Daddy, I promise I won't do it. Please tell me how it's done.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told my wife, who was very educated, I said, you handle it. We went to child guidance. You tell them to take off. She said, you started something, you finish it. We are fighting amongst each other who is going to handle such a thing. They were [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't ask no question, neither did the dog. Just Marcia was so inquisitive. Daddy, I promise I won't do it. I said, if you do it, I'll kill you, because you know what do I think I survived the war for. So she kept saying, Daddy, I promise I won't do it. That's all I want know. How did the flame come out the paper? So you see, in a child's mind is different than an adult. We see it differently. You're asking about me being afraid. It's you taking it with the punches. You were just a small boy, and you were all alone during that time. Weren't you feeling lonely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to play cards with one hand-- the other. I would play war with one hand with the other one. I used to sing songs on the fields. I used to be in the fields, tending cow, let's say. The minute I got bored, I didn't want to tend no cow. So I would round up a bunch of fruit. I used to steal off the trees at night when a peasant was sleeping. Sneak in somebody's barn and sleep there. For months, weeks, days-- I don't know how long I stayed there. And the pears ripened in the hay. And I used to sleep there. And if I was thirsty, I used to milk the cow in my mouth, come down at night and milk the cow in my mouth. And I used [INAUDIBLE] steal the raw eggs. Chicken, when they lay an egg, their nest all over the place. They're running around loose, chickens in Poland-- probably today, too. And I used to drink the warm eggs only. So the chicken would come back. The lady of the house would come back collecting her basket of [INAUDIBLE] eggs. There are no eggs because I drank it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the husband and wife they are fighting. They're much better taking care of the cat, the dog, or it's a skunk is maybe stealing the eggs. And I did it in various different places. I did the same thing over and over again. You would sneak onto farms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Into the farmer's barn or the silo.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would sneak into the barns.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sleep there. In the silo there was a tunnel. I'd sleep in the tunnel there. And at night, just go out and relieve myself or steal fruit off the trees at night. Shake the trees, and fruit will fall to the ground, and put it inside my shirt and go back in the haystack and sleep there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the farmers never knew that you were in their barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they would catch me, they would kill me-- not only as a Jew, but as a thief, as an intruder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep in the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the haystack. I dug a hole, like, in the haystack. And at times-- one time I dug myself a hole in the forest. And I put lots of hay on the bottom as a foundation. It was fall. Without realizing it was fall, and I collected lots of straw. I built, like, a roof with branches. And I saved lots of fruit-- raw, really green-- those Italian plums-- very green, green apples, green pears, and I lived off it. And there was snow on the ground, frost. But the frost doesn't reach below. The ground only freezes a certain amount. I stayed there for a long, long time. I ran out of food, I went back again to the trains.
